surprise_finding: Cardiac pacemaker control via the FitzHugh-Nagumo model exhibits an asymmetric learning landscape: PPO rapidly learns to avoid harmful over-pacing (negative reward avoidance) but struggles with the subtler task of precisely matching target heart rate variability. This mirrors clinical observations where crude rate control is achievable but physiological rate-responsive pacing requires sophisticated sensing and adaptation. The reward shaping that bridges this gap -- penalizing HRV deviation alongside rate error -- creates a curriculum effect where agents first stabilize rate, then refine variability, paralleling the clinical programming workflow of bradycardia pacemakers.
implications: RL benchmarking for sequential cardiac decision-making
Curriculum learning emergent from physiological reward structure
